## [00:00 - 03:07]

In this training, we're dealing with something that most people encounter when they try something new. And that is the dreaded imposter syndrome. For those of you who don't know what imposter syndrome is, no, it's not when you become a spy. It is a feeling that most people get, like I said, when they try something new, that anxiety that they're afraid or fake and that they would be found out any minute or any second now. It's a little bit like this picture over there, where we're trying to fit in and we're taking all the steps, we're walking the walk, but it just doesn't feel that we're owning it, so we're trying to blend in. Let's have a look at who suffers from imposter syndrome. I found this really funny little image of types of people who can have imposter syndrome, and that's all the smart, successful people that you think have their shit together. And it's true. I have been amazed, no, shocked at some people that I speak to who are really respected and they feel really successful. They just seem like they've got it together all the time. And then when you talk to them a little bit deeper on a deeper level, they reveal that they too feel like a fraud or an imposter at times, and that's really, really revealing. So the first thing if you've ever, ever experienced imposter syndrome is to understand that you're not alone. It's completely, completely fine and normal. However, in this training, we are going to unpack exactly where it's coming from and what you can do to overcome it so that it doesn't stop you from going after what you really want. Okay? There's four elements that are in imposter syndrome. Okay? Imposter, there's four. Do a little mind map. Number one is that there's lots and lots of... What's the word? Uncertainting. So can you identify if you ever have experienced imposter syndrome that there's, at the core, it's more so of you, you're not sure of what it's going to look like on the other side. So the real core of imposter syndrome is a ton of uncertainty. You know, is this going to work? Is this going to work for me? Am I good enough for this? You know, all those kind of dots come in and there are uncertainties around something new and that's also completely normal. Now, if we choose to relabel imposter syndrome into uncertainty syndrome, it takes off a

## [03:07 - 06:11]

lot of the negative connotation that imposter syndrome has. Like unless you're a spy, being imposter isn't... It's frowned upon, right? And it's not something that we aim for. However, uncertainty is much more neutral because uncertainty isn't good nor is it bad. It simply is. So if we have a more neutral understanding of this feeling that we get, then it's much easier to do something about it. Because when we know that it's uncertainty, we can ask ourselves questions like, how can you bring more certainty in the situation? Or how can I bring more certainty into my life in general so that the situation doesn't feel so tubeless, right? So that's the very first one of imposter syndrome and that's uncertainty. Number two is a lack of confidence. It's a lack of confidence. Can you see that? And I work with people around confidence all the time. It's one of my favorite, favorite topics to teach because there's a huge misunderstanding about what confidence really is. A lot of people see other people as confident and identify that they themselves don't have this so-called confidence, but they don't actually know what it is. Let me give you one of my favorite definitions of confidence. Okay? Okay? Repetitive courageous actions, all right? Repetitive courageous actions towards less uncertainty. Okay? Now the great thing about this definition of confidence is that it's not out there. Something that is intangible and it's not for you. It's something that you can break down and actually take upon yourself to do something about it. So the elements here are that it's repetitive. So it's something that you do time and time again. There's no one who's doing something for the very first time that's out the gate confident in doing that. It's something they have repeated before and now have a certain understanding, a certain confidence around it. Okay? Number two is that it's courageous actions. Courage is much more important than confidence because behind all confidence is a ton of repetitive courageous actions until there is ultimately less uncertainty.

## [06:11 - 09:17]

Okay? Can you see when you have these three elements that you can gain more confidence? And with this model of confidence, you have the power to do something about it versus of being this intangible thing that nobody really understands. Okay? So that is how we can gain more confidence. Okay? And then number three of imposter syndrome is where we severely devalue ourselves. Okay? When we try something new or are learning how to price ourselves better or learning a new business model, learning anything new, it feels sometimes that it's all new and we forget all the things that we've done in our lives and our businesses up until this point to get here. So we forget how resourceful we are, for example. We forget all the courageous steps that we've taken before. We forget all the people we've met and the networks that we've built up that are there to support us. And that is really also, it kind of propels that impulsive feeling or that uncertainty feeling because all of a sudden there's all this newness and we don't have an anchor of how to then value ourselves. Now a very big part in valuing ourselves is our belief in our resourcefulness. Okay? In short, resourcefulness is the... Hold on one second. Sourcefulness is the ability to solve problems. Okay? Now, if you have the ability to solve problems and if you are resourceful, then you can start building that value around this newness and uncertainty that you have because then whatever happens, you have the power to do something about it. You have that power and the tools to solve the problems and to figure it out. And when we truly understand that everything, we can figure anything out that we're truly resourceful, we have people around us that can help us. We have tools around us. We have the internet with like so many resources and everything and we are able to solve problems with our resourcefulness then that impulsive syndrome starts lifting off more and more. And finally, the final aspect of impulsive syndrome is that those butterflies, that nervousness

## [09:17 - 11:16]

that we feel is also the same feeling when we have something new and exciting. So it's very much a symptom of something happening that's new and exciting and that's like really cool because if we have this feeling and then we choose to give it a different meaning, we choose to give it a meaning of something new and exciting happening versus oh, I'm not good enough or I'm a fraud, then that's a very empowered state to be. So next time, next time you feel yourself like this impulsive syndrome is coming on, I urge you to ask yourself this one question. What meaning am I giving it? Am I giving it the meaning that I'm not good enough and I'm a fraud and everyone else is better than me or can I choose to give it a different meaning? Can I choose to give it a meaning that I'm going to gain more certainty over time? Okay, I'm going to build confidence over time. I'm going to get more and more resourceful and valuable. And actually, this is a great sign because it's new and exciting. Right? So that is the choice that we have when dealing with impulsive syndrome.
